reasoning: The sink is confirmed: every SVG case in getIcon() does `echo $class;` bare inside the SVG class HTML attribute with no esc_attr() or esc_html() call. However, $class is NOT reachable with attacker-controlled input through any call path in the codebase. All direct callers of getIcon() and getMagnifierIco() pass hardcoded string literals as the $class argument. The $iconType shortcode attribute ($args['icon']) flows to the $type/$name parameter of getMagnifierIco/getIcon (the switch-case selector), not to $class. The $iconColor shortcode attribute flows to the $color parameter, which is properly escaped with esc_attr() at every usage site. The apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/icon', $svg, $name, $class, $color) call passes $class as a read-only informational argument; filter callbacks receive the already-rendered $svg string and can modify it, but this does not allow an attacker to retroactively inject into the already-built SVG via $class. Adding a filter hook requires admin/code-level access, which is out-of-scope for a plugin vulnerability. The Blocks.php entry point sanitizes icon_color with esc_attr() before passing it to the shortcode. The widget.php does not pass icon_color at all. There is a latent/theoretical code quality issue (missing escaping on $class), but no exploitable path from any user-controlled input to the $class sink exists in the current codebase.
